coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF for the sensitive detection of riboflavin via FRET
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Shasha
Pei, Fubin
Wu, Yi
Lv, Jingjing
Hao, Qingli
Yang, Tinghai
Tong, Zhaoyang
Lei, Wu
coredata.dc:description: A novel ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) platform was designed for riboflavin (RF) detection. The graphitic carbon nitrides quantum dots - Zn-MOF composite (g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF) was used as the fluorescent probe. In the FRET system, g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF and RF acted as donor and acceptor, respectively. The probe exhibited high sensitivity and good selectivity to RF, and had been successfully used for the detection of RF in milk and vitamin B2 tablets. The detection limit of the sensor was 15 nM. The strategy expanded the application of MOF in sensing filed and provided a new method for the detection of RF.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric
Fluorescence detection
g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF
Riboflavin

TEM characterization of g-CNQDs, Zn-MOF and g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF
FTIR characterization of g-CNQDs, Zn-MOF and g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF
Comparison of fluorescence intensity of Zn-MOF, g-CNQDs and g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF
Effect of response time and pH

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21576138 , 51872140 , 51972173 ), and the Program for Science and Technology Innovative Research Team in Universities of Jiangsu Province, China . We also thank the support of the Analysis and Test Center, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, for the FT-IR and UV–vis absorption spectra data collection.

The work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21576138, 51872140, 51972173), and the Program for Science and Technology Innovative Research Team in Universities of Jiangsu Province, China. We also thank the support of the Analysis and Test Center, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, for the FT-IR and UV–vis absorption spectra data collection.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of g-CNQDs (A), Zn-MOF (B) and g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF (C and D).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of Zn-MOF, g-CNQDs and g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF; (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of QDs (g-CNQDs) and QDs@MOF (g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF) without and with 5 μM RF (The preparation of Zn-MOF and g-CNQDs was similar to that of g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF, but without adding each other. The excitation wavelength was 365 nm and the slit was 3 nm).
(A) Excitation-emission matrix fluorescence spectra of g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF; (B) Fluorescence spectra of g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF at different excitation wavelengths (345 nm, 355 nm, 365 nm, 375 nm, and 385 nm).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF in the presence of various concentrations of RF (0.005, 0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 1.0 μM); (B) Linearity relationship between the fluorescence ratio (I520nm/I450nm) of g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF and RF concentration (pH = 7, λex = 365 nm). The error bars represent standard deviations based on three independent measurements.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of RF (a), fluorescence excitation (b) and fluorescence emission spectrum (c) of g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF; (B) Fluorescence lifetime of g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF with different concentrations of RF (0, 1, 5, 10, 15, 20 μM).
UV–vis absorption spectra of g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF with different concentrations of RF.
Schematic of fabrication of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on FRET (H2BDC: 1,4-benzenedicarboxylic acid, TED: 1,4-diazabicyclo [2.2.2] octane).
Comparison of sensing performances for RF of various fluorescent probes based on FRET.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: N, S-CDs: N, S doped carbon dots; AHNPs: 9-anthradehdye derivative nanoparticles; NPCDs: nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The fluorescence lifetime of g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF in the presence of RF.
The recoveries of RF concentration detection in real samples.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF for the sensitive detection of riboflavin via FRET

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing 210094, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanjing University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing 210094, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry & Environmental Engineering, Jiangsu University of Technology, Changzhou 213001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry & Environmental Engineering, Jiangsu University of Technology, Changzhou 213001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian, Beijing 102205, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian, Beijing 102205, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) platform was designed for riboflavin (RF) detection. The graphitic carbon nitrides quantum dots - Zn-MOF composite (g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF) was used as the fluorescent probe. In the FRET system, g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF and RF acted as donor and acceptor, respectively. The probe exhibited high sensitivity and good selectivity to RF, and had been successfully used for the detection of RF in milk and vitamin B2 tablets. The detection limit of the sensor was 15 nM. The strategy expanded the application of MOF in sensing filed and provided a new method for the detection of RF.

ce:section.ce:para: Riboflavin (RF), also known as vitamin B2, plays a key role in the human body. RF can assist fat, protein and carbohydrates to be converted into energy in the body [1]. RF is water-soluble, which can be committed to forming two coenzymes, flavin mononucleotide (FMN) and flavin adenine dinucleotide (FAD) [2]. Moreover, RF can participate in many cellular processes, such as nucleic acid repair processes, apoptosis, and electron transfer processes in the respiratory chain [3,4]. RF cannot be produced from the human body and can only be supplied through food or medicine [5]. Lack or excess of riboflavin in the body is harmful, so it is essential for the detection of RF. At present, there are many methods for RF detection, including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6], HPLC–tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) [7], capillary electrophoresis [8], fluorescence [5], colorimetry [9] and electrochemical methods [10]. Among these methods, fluorescence analysis is popular owing to its high sensitivity, good selectivity, simple operation, real-time detection and good reproducibility. Compared with single-emission fluorescent strategy, the ratiometric fluorescent strategy can eliminate the external interference of environmental elements through the self-calibration of two emission bands, which can improve the accuracy of the sensor [11,12].

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are a new type of porous material and the framework structure is composed of a large number of available metal ions and organic ligands [13]. MOFs possess a lot of unique properties, such as large specific surface area, abundant functionality, extremely high of porosity, tunable structure and size, ordered crystal structure and good stability [14,15]. Therefore, MOFs have been used in many fields, such as catalysis [16], drug delivery and release [17], gas storage [18], ion exchange [19] and sensing [20]. In recent years, a lot of research has focused on the luminescence phenomenon of MOFs. MOFs can represent outstanding luminescent properties, which come from π - electron rich conjugated organic ligands, metal ions/clusters, emissive guest molecules and so on [21]. The cubic network structure of Zn-MOF can facilitate charges transfer and adsorb the target analytes selectively. The luminescent Zn-MOF can absorb the light with the wavelengths greater than 350 nm, which significantly improves the utilization of light source [22]. The particularity of MOF structure allows it to be effectively combined with other materials to overcome its own shortcomings, such as low transmission efficiency of photo-generated electrons and wide band gap [23]. The emissive guest molecules can be some functional materials, including semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), carbon dots, organic dyes and metal nanoclusters [24]. The fluorescence intensity of MOFs can be enhanced when compounded with other luminescent guest molecules. A dual-emitting platform can also be originated from the system. The emission scope and detectable species are expanded simultaneously in the system, while structure and properties of MOF stay unchanged [25]. This is an important method to synthesize ideal functional luminescence MOFs. So far, many new functional MOFs composites have been prepared by introducing various quantum dots into the MOFs as the guest. This type of fluorescence sensing platform constructed by the composite material that combines the excellent performance of the host-MOFs and the guest-quantum dots has good fluorescence performance, exhibiting good selectivity and high sensitivity. Graphitic carbon nitrides quantum dots (g-CNQDs) are the carbon material rich in N with the graphitized structure [26]. g-CNQDs have attracted much attention for their advantages of good optical properties, low toxicity, water solubility, good biocompatibility and chemical stability [27,28]. To our knowledge, the application of g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF in sensing filed has been rarely reported. Due to the introduction of Zn-MOF, g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF not only has good fluorescence performance, but also can improve the sensitivity and selectivity of the sensor to RF.

In this work, a novel ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF was proposed to detect RF through FRET for the first time. Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) is a process in which energy is transferred from an excited fluorophore (donor) to another fluorophore (acceptor) through a dipole-dipole interaction in a non-radiative manner [29]. In this work, the energy was transferred from g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF to RF. Due to the low background signal, the ratiometric detection method based on FRET greatly improved the accuracy and sensitivity of the sensor to the target analyte. Scheme 1 represented the fabrication of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor.

Sodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7·2H2O), Urea, Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (H12N2O12Zn), 1,4-benzenedicarboxylic acid (H2BDC, C8H6O4), 1,4-diazabicyclo [2.2.2] octane (TED, C6H12N2), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), riboflavin (RF, C17H20N4O6), vitamin B9 (VB9), vitamin B3 (VB3), lysine (Lys), L-aspartic acid monosodium (ASP), and uric acid (UA) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China), while sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), dibasic sodium phosphate (Na2HPO4), glucose (Glu), ascorbic acid (AA), dopamine (DA), glutathione (GSH), KCl, NaCl and CaCl2 were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Vitamin B2 tablets were purchased from Beijing Zhongxin Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd.. All analytical-grade reagents were used without further purification. 0.2 M PBS buffer solutions with different pH value were composed of 0.2 M NaH2PO4 solution and 0.2 M Na2HPO4 solution in different proportion.

The TEM images were measured by JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were carried out on Nicolet iS50 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). The UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained by a UV-220 spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). All photoluminescence spectra and fluorescence lifetime were measured on FL3-TCSPC fluorescence spectrophotometer (HORIBA Jobin Yvon, France).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of g-CNQDs, Zn-MOF and g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF
ce:section.ce:para: g-CNQDs are synthesized according to previously reported literature [30]. 0.101 g (1.68 mmol) urea and 0.081 g (0.28 mmol) sodium citrate were mixed and ground into a uniform powder in an agate mortar. Then, the mixture was placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave and kept in an oven at 180 °C for 1 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, the yellow product was washed with ethanol and centrifuged at 12000 rpm/min for three times. The g-CNQDs were obtained by dialyzing the above yellow product for 24 h with the dialysis membrane (retained molecular weight 1000 Da) in pure water.

Zn-MOF was prepared by the hydrothermal method [31]. Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (0.58 g, 2 mmol), H2BDC (0.35 g, 2 mmol) and TED (0.11 g, 1 mmol) were dissolved in 20, 10 and 20 mL DMF, respectively. Then, H2BDC and TED solution were dropped into zinc nitrate hexahydrate solution. The mixture was placed to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 24 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, the product was separated by filtration and washed three times with 10 mL DMF, which was heated at 120 °C under N2 flow for one day to remove DMF molecules.

5 mg Zn-MOF was dispersed in 25 mL 0.2 M PBS buffer solution and sonicated for 1 h. 300 μL g-CNQDs were added to the above solution, and the mixture was sonicated for 6 h to obtain g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF solution.
The volume of the g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF probe solution was fixed to 2 mL, and different concentrations of RF were sequentially added to the g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF solution for fluorescence testing.

Fluorescence performance measurements: Unless otherwise specified in this experiment, the parameters of the photoluminescence (PL) test were as follows: excitation wavelength was 365 nm, excitation wavelength slit was 3, emission wavelength range was 380–700 nm, emission wavelength slit was 3, step length was 0.5 nm. The fluorescence lifetime test was carried out in the transient state, and the excitation source was 371 nm without special explanation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pretreatment and analysis of actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Bottled milk was purchased from the local supermarket. According to the reference [32], 16 mL milk was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min to separate the adipose layer. The upper liquid was collected as a stock solution and stored at 4 °C. Vitamin B2 tablets were ground into powder and dissolved in 16 mL water. The solution was centrifuged to remove the undissolved powder at 8000 rpm for 15 min, and the upper solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane filter to remove large particles. Finally, the saturated riboflavin solution was collected as an actual sample and stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TEM characterization of g-CNQDs, Zn-MOF and g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 presented the TEM images of g-CNQDs (A), MOF (B) and g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF (C and D). It can be seen from Fig. 1A that the prepared g-CNQDs were well-shaped spherical with 20 nm. And the Zn-MOF was bulk (Fig. 1B). Fig. 1C and D displayed the TEM images of g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF at different magnification. Compared with Zn-MOF, the morphology of g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF hardly changed. TEM results showed that the doping of g-CNQDs did not affect the main structure of Zn-MOF, and g-CNQDs were successfully encapsulated in Zn-MOF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FTIR characterization of g-CNQDs, Zn-MOF and g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF
ce:section.ce:para: The functional groups of the prepared materials were examined by FTIR characterization. Fig. S1 showed the FTIR spectra of Zn-MOF (a), g-CNQDs (b) and g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF (c). For Zn-MOF (Fig. S1a), the absorption peak of 1307 cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibration peak of CN in MOF. The absorption peaks of 1371, 1501 and 1567 cm−1 were the characteristic peaks of COO stretching vibration in Zn-MOF. The broad absorption peaks between 2950 cm−1 and 3350 cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibration of CH2 and NH in MOF. As can be seen from Fig. S1b, the absorption peak of 1454 cm−1 belonged to the typical stretching vibration peak of CN in g-CNQDs, the strong absorption peak at 1592 cm−1 corresponded to the asymmetric stretching vibration of CO in the carboxylate anion, and the wide absorption peaks between 3090 cm−1 and 3452 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of NH and OH in g-CNQDs. For g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF, it can be seen from Fig. S1c that the characteristic absorption peaks of both g-CNQDs and Zn-MOF appeared. And the asymmetric stretching vibration peak of CO in the carboxylate anion red-shifted to 1501 cm−1, the stretching vibration peak of CN and the stretching vibration peak of COO blue-shifted to 1587 cm−1, 1468 cm−1 and 1377 cm−1, respectively. The result demonstrated some strong hydrogen bonds were formed between g-CNQDs and Zn-MOF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of fluorescence intensity of Zn-MOF, g-CNQDs and g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence emission spectra of Zn-MOF, g-CNQDs and g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF were shown in Fig. 2 . As can be seen from Fig. 2, the maximum emission wavelengths of Zn-MOF and g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF were 424 nm and 450 nm, respectively. The fluorescence intensity of Zn-MOF was the weakest and that of g-CNQDs was the strongest (Fig. 2A). But the sensitivity of g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF to RF ((I520nm/I450nm)1 − (I520nm/I450nm)0 = 6.464) was more better than that of g-CNQDs ((I520nm/I450nm)1ˊ − (I520nm/I450nm)0ˊ = 5.347), where 1 and 0 represented the presence and absence of RF, respectively (Fig. 2B). It may be due to the high adsorption of MOF to RF. The result indicated that Zn-MOF can effectively improve the sensing performance of g-CNQDs.

The UV–vis absorbance spectra of g-CNQDs (a), g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF (b) and Zn-MOF (c) were recorded in Fig. S2. As shown in Fig. S2A, the prepared Zn-MOF and g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF produced strong absorbance peaks at 240 nm. Due to the low concentration of g-CNQDs, the characteristics absorbance peak of the g-CNQDs was not obvious. The absorbance spectra of g-CNQDs and g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF overlapped completely between 300 and 600 nm, and the absorbance peak of g-CNQDs was located at 323 nm (the inset of Fig. S2A), while 0.12 mg/mL g-CNQDs exhibited an absorbance peak at 323 nm (As presented in Fig. S2B). These results indicated that the g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF was prepared successfully.

In order to explore the luminescent properties of the prepared g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF, the three-dimensional (3D) excitation-emission matrix (Fig. 3A) and fluorescence emission fluorescence spectra (Fig. 3B) of g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF at different excitation wavelengths (360–380 nm) were tested, and the range of emission wavelength was 440–460 nm. The maximum emission wavelength was in the red region, and the corresponding optimum excitation wavelength was 365 nm. The excitation dependent fluorescence emission spectra (Fig. 3B) were recorded to explore the changing trend of fluorescence intensity of g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF under different excitation wavelengths. It can be seen from Fig. 3B that the fluorescence intensity of g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF reached the highest value at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm. With the increase of excitation wavelength, the fluorescence spectra gradually red-shifted, which indicated g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF had an excitation-dependent fluorescent characteristic [33–35]. Therefore, the optimal excitation wavelength was 365 nm. And the fluorescence quantum yields (QYs) of g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF was 35% (The details of the calculation were shown in the Supplementary material).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of response time and pH
ce:section.ce:para: After adding 15 μM RF, the change of fluorescence intensity ratio (I520nm/I450nm) of g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF with the response time was recorded in Fig. S3A, wherein I450nm and I520nm were the fluorescence intensity of g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF (donor) and RF (acceptor), respectively. As can be seen that the fluorescence intensity ratio had basically no change within 30 min, indicating that the fluorescence intensity was not affected by the response time and g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF could respond rapidly toward RF. Hence, the reaction was real-time.

Generally, the pH of the detection system solution will affect the sensitivity and selectivity of the sensor. The pH values (5.8–7.8) were selected to study the influence of different pH on detection efficiency. As can be seen from Fig. S3B, the detection efficiency of the probe with different pH values changed little in the presence of RF, indicating that under a wide pH range, the probe solution in the presence of RF existed steadily. The experimental result showed that the constructed fluorescent sensor was of great significance for real sample detection. The pH value of g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF solution during the experiment was selected as 7.

As shown in Fig. 4A, the fluorescence intensity ratio I520nm/I450nm of probe solution was recorded with different concentrations of RF solution to evaluate the sensitivity of the sensor. With the increment of addition RF concentration, the fluorescence intensity decreased at 450 nm and a new emission peak increased at 520 nm. Meanwhile, Fig. 4B showed that the good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (I520nm/I450nm) of g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF and various concentrations of RF (0.005–1.0 μM) was obtained. The linear equation was determined to be I520nm/I450nm = 0.594·CRF + 0.404 (R2 = 0.998).

According to the calculation formula LOD = 3Sd/k, the LOD of g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF fluorescent probe to RF was calculated to be 15 nM (S/N = 3), where Sd was the standard deviation and k was the slope of the linear equation. Compared with sensing performances for RF of various fluorescent probes based on FRET (Table 1 ), it can be concluded that the LOD of the proposed strategy was relatively low. Due to its porous structure and large specific surface area, MOF can be loaded with QDs to prevent the aggregation of QDs. The sensitivity and selectivity of the sensor increase due to the highly selective adsorption of MOF to RF [36]. Therefore, the proposed sensor has a good application prospect.

The mechanism of fluorescence quenching includes static and dynamic quenching. Fluorescence quenching process can be divided into inner filter effect (IFE), Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and photo-induced electron transfer (PET), etc., among which IFE and FRET are common.

Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) is a non-radiative process in which an excited donor molecule transfers energy to the ground state acceptor molecule through dipole-dipole interaction. The efficiency of energy transfer depends largely on the relative direction of the two dipoles and the degree of the spectral overlap between the donor emission spectrum and the acceptor absorption spectrum. As shown in Fig. 5A, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of RF exhibited absorption at 200–500 nm, and four obvious UV absorption peaks at 222, 266, 372 and 446 nm (curve a), respectively. The optimal excitation wavelength of g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF was observed at 365 nm, while a strong fluorescence emission peak was shown at 450 nm under the excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The fluorescence excitation spectrum (curve b) and emission spectrum (curve c) of g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF greatly overlapped with the UV–vis absorption spectrum of RF. The results revealed that there was a great possibility of FRET between g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF (donor) and RF (acceptor). To further prove that FRET occurred between g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF and RF, rather than an inner filter effect (IEF), the fluorescence lifetimes of g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF without RF and with different concentrations of RF were recorded in Fig. 5B. With the increase of RF concentration, the fluorescence lifetime of the solution decreased gradually, and the lifetime values (τ) were shown in Table 2 . Since the IFE process does not change the lifetime of g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF, the results excluded the possibility of IFE between g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF and RF. Therefore, FRET can be considered as the main cause of fluorescence change of g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF.

In order to further explore the quenching mechanism, the UV–vis absorbance spectra of the g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF solution with different concentrations of RF were studied, as shown in Fig. 6 . With the increase of RF concentration, the absorbance intensity of g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF increased gradually, and the new absorption peak did not appear. The phenomenon indicated that there was weak interaction between g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF and RF, so there was no formation of new compounds. The reaction process was dominated by dynamic quenching. From the results, the absorbance of g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF probe solution increased in the presence of RF. It can be further inferred that RF improved the intermolecular energy transfer efficiency and non-radiation decay rate, resulting in a decrease of lifetime of g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF.

In order to have a deeper understanding of FRET process, the Förster distance (R0) was caclulated based on Eq. (1) [39]:
(1)
R 0 6
= 8.79 ×
10
− 28
mol ×
n
− 4
k 2
QJ
(2)
J = ∫
F D
λ
ε A
λ
λ 4
dλ / ∫
F D
λ
dλ
where n (1.33) is the refractive index of the solvent, k2 (2/3) is the orientation factor, and J is the spectral overlap integral (Fig. S4). It can be obtained by calculation that J was 3.15 × 10−15 cm3 M−1 and R0 was 5.9 nm within the range (1–10 nm) required by FRET.

In fluorescence detection, many inorganic ions and organic matters will interfere with the detection effect of the sensor. In order to evaluate the selectivity of the proposed detection method for riboflavin, various interfering species were added, including 50 μM vitamin B9 (VB9), vitamin B3 (VB3), glucose (Glu), lysine (Lys), aspartic acid (ASP), uric acid (UA), ascorbic acid (AA), dopamine (DA), glutathione (GSH), Na+, K+ and Ca2+. As shown in Fig. S5, the F/F0 values were around 1.0, indicating that the I520nm/I450nm values of the g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF solution had no obvious variation before and after the addition of interfering substances, while F0 and F represented the fluorescence intensity ratio (I520nm/I450nm) without and with interfering substances, respectively. There were negligible effects on the fluorescence responses of g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF, indicating the fluorescent probe had a good anti-interference ability.

In the experiment, the stability of the g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF fluorescent probe was tested. The fluorescence intensity g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF probe stored at room temperature was tested once a day for 10 consecutive days. Fig. 7 displayed the initial and final fluorescence intensity of g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF fluorescent probe within 10 days. The fluorescence intensity remained almost unchanged, indicating that g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF had good stability.

In order to explore the practicality of the sensor, the g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF was applied to detect the concentration of riboflavin in milk and vitamin B2 tablets. The standard addition method was employed for real sample detection. Satisfactory results were obtained in Table 3 with the recovery rate between 98.8% and 106.0%, and the RSD between 0.35% and 1.02%. These results demonstrated that the constructed fluorescence probe was very meaningful for real sample analysis.

In this work, a novel FRET-based ratiometric fluorescent probe was proposed for the sensitive detection of riboflavin, in which g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF fluorescence probe and RF played donor and acceptor, respectively. The g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF was prepared by self-assembling g-CNQDs into Zn-MOF, which combined the advantages of both g-CNQDs and Zn-MOF, so the g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF fluorescent probe had a strong and rapid fluorescence response to riboflavin. The sensing response mechanism between g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF and riboflavin was studied. Under the optimal experimental conditions, g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF fluorescence probe had a lower detection limit, excellent selectivity and stability for RF detection. In addition, the probe had been successfully used for the rapid and sensitive determination of RF in milk and vitamin B2 tablets. The method expanded the application of MOF in chemo/bio-sensor and provided a novel method for the detection of riboflavin.

Shasha Feng: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Fubin Pei: Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Yi Wu: Investigation, Software. Jingjing Lv: Validation, Methodology. Qingli Hao: Resources, Funding acquisition. Tinghai Yang: Visualization. Zhaoyang Tong: Project administration, Writing - review & editing. Wu Lei: Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21576138, 51872140, 51972173), and the Program for Science and Technology Innovative Research Team in Universities of Jiangsu Province, China. We also thank the support of the Analysis and Test Center, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, for the FT-IR and UV–vis absorption spectra data collection.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.119004.
